name: Cosmological lithium problem
summary: Standard Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) with the CMB-determined baryon density predicts a primordial Li-7 abundance Li-7/H ~ 5e-10, but observed Li-7 abundances in old metal-poor halo stars (the 'Spite plateau') are a factor of 3-4 lower at Li-7/H ~ 1.6e-10. The discrepancy persists at ~5 sigma after improved nuclear-rate measurements; D/H and He-4 agree with BBN beautifully, isolating Li-7 as the outlier.

characteristic_numbers.note: BBN with Omega_b h^2 = 0.0223 (Planck) and current nuclear rates.
~3x lower than BBN prediction at >5 sigma significance after Cyburt-Fields-Olive 2008 update.

principles_stressed.note: Either (a) systematic in stellar Li depletion (turbulent mixing in old stars destroying Li), (b) systematic in BBN reaction rates (3He(α,γ)7Be cross-section), or (c) genuine new physics (decaying particles, modified expansion rate, varying constants during BBN). Decades of work have not resolved which.

history: Spite plateau measured in 1982 (Spite & Spite). Discrepancy with BBN sharpened progressively as CMB pinned down Omega_b. Cyburt, Fields, Olive (2008), 'An update on the big bang nucleosynthesis prediction for 7Li: the problem worsens', JCAP 11, 012, arXiv:0808.2818, found the gap widening. Modern reviews: Fields (2011), 'The primordial lithium problem', Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 61, 47, arXiv:1203.3551.

references.citation: Cyburt, Fields, Olive, 'An update on the big bang nucleosynthesis prediction for 7Li: the problem worsens', JCAP 11, 012 (2008)
Fields, 'The primordial lithium problem', Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 61, 47 (2011)
